basmala: In the Name of God, the Infinitely Merciful, the Most Merciful.
intro: Al-Falaq — “The Daybreak” — is the one hundred and thirteenth sūra of the Quran. Together with the following sūra, an-Nās, it forms the Muʿawwidhatān, “the two protectresses”: the two sūras of refuge. Tradition recounts that they were revealed in response to a spell cast upon the Prophet — a knotted cord over which malevolent words had been whispered. The archangel Gabriel disclosed where the cord was hidden, and the two sūras — fourteen verses for fourteen knots — were recited over it to undo the bewitchment. To this day Muslims use them as words of protection against many evils. But their reach is broader: al-Falaq guards against the evils that come from without — the night that advances, the knotted word of sorcerers, the envy that consumes — while an-Nās guards the inner place, the heart, against the whisper of the tempter.

versets.trad: Say: “I take refuge with the Lord of the daybreak,
versets.commentaire: al-falaq (الفَلَق): the daybreak, the first day that splits open from the night. The word comes from the verb falaqa (فَلَقَ), “to cleave” — God is called elsewhere fāliq al-iṣbāḥ (فَالِق الْإِصْبَاح), “the Cleaver of the dawn” (6:96). The image speaks of the rending of darkness by light. But al-falaq has a wider sense: that which breaks forth, which appears — including truth that emerges from uncertainty. To seek refuge with the Lord of the daybreak is therefore to take the side of light against shadow, of the true against the false. The Quran says elsewhere: “Truth has come, and falsehood has vanished” (17:81).

mots.def: to say, to utter, to pronounce; in the imperative: “say”, “proclaim”.
to seek protection, to take refuge with someone so as to be guarded from a danger or an evil.
rabb: the Lord, the Master; the one who owns a thing, nourishes it, raises it and brings it to its full growth.
mots.gram: preposition bi- + noun in the genitive

mots.def: the dawn that cleaves the night, the appearing of the day; in a wider sense, that which breaks forth — including truth emerging from uncertainty. From the root f-l-q, “to cleave”.

versets.trad: from the evil of what He has created,
versets.commentaire: The formula is deliberately broad: min sharri mā khalaq (مِنْ شَرِّ مَا خَلَقَ), “from the evil of what He has created”. It can refer to Satan and his kind, to the fire of Hell, or simply to whatever in creation may cause harm. Some commentators also read it as a veil: creation itself, which both conceals the Creator and reveals His Qualities. Under whatever form evil takes, the believer seeks shelter with the very One who creates — not in some other power, but in the sole Lord, to whom all being belongs.

mots.def: preposition: from, out of; here, the idea of guarding oneself from, of protection against.
evil, harm; that which is bad or harmful — the opposite of good (khayr).
neuter relative pronoun: what, that which; here referring to the whole of what has been created.
khalaqa: to create, to shape, to bring into being from a design; implicit subject: God.

versets.trad: from the evil of darkness as it gathers,
versets.commentaire: ghāsiq idhā waqab (غَاسِقٍ إِذَا وَقَبَ): darkness when it gathers. The verb waqaba (وَقَبَ) carries the sense of penetrating, slipping in, spreading slowly. Most commentators see in it the falling of night — the hour when, says the Quran elsewhere, “those who plot by night plot” (4:81). A saying of the Prophet, looking at the moon, also applies the word to it: “O ʿĀʾisha, seek refuge in God from this one, this is al-ghāsiq (الغَاسِق) when it declines.” Darkness here is whatever advances by covering — the night without, and the night that may fall upon a life.

mots.def: coordinating conjunction: and; joins two terms or two clauses.
preposition: from, out of; here, the idea of protection against.
evil, harm; that which is bad or harmful.
that which grows dark; refers first to the falling night, sometimes also to the declining moon. From the root gh-s-q.
temporal particle: when, at the moment that.
waqaba: to penetrate, to slip in, to spread; in speaking of night, to advance and envelop everything.

versets.trad: from the evil of those who blow upon knots,
versets.commentaire: an-naffāthāt fī l-ʿuqad (النَّفَّاثَاتِ فِي الْعُقَدِ): those who blow upon knots. Tradition reports the precise image — a knotted cord over which incantations are whispered. This is the very background of the sūra's revelation. More broadly the expression names all those — women or men, the feminine plural is not exclusive — who practise sorcery. The verb nafatha (نَفَثَ), “to blow out, to expel a little breath”, evokes the rite: a thread is tied, a word is breathed, and a will is claimed to be bound. Against this gesture, in which human breath arrogates a power, the only shelter is the word of God.

mots.def: coordinating conjunction: and.
preposition: from, out of; here, the idea of protection against.
evil, harm; that which is bad or harmful.
naffāthāt: those who blow while whispering, who practise bewitchment by breath; intensive form of the verb nafatha, “to blow out a word”.
preposition: in, into, upon.
ʿuqda: a knot, a binding; in the plural, the knots — such as those of a cord used in casting a spell. From the root ʿ-q-d.

versets.trad: and from the evil of the envier when he envies.”
versets.commentaire: ḥāsid idhā ḥasad (حَاسِدٍ إِذَا حَسَدَ): the envier when he envies. Envy — ḥasad (حَسَد) — is not the mere wish to have what another possesses: it is the deeper evil of wishing the other to be stripped of it. The Caliph ʿUmar ibn ʿAbd al-ʿAzīz put it this way: “I cannot think of a wrongdoer more wronged than the one who envies another.” From one perspective, envy is the root of every evil: the first sin in heaven was Iblīs's envy of Adam, and the first on earth was Cain's envy of Abel. The envier in truth sets himself against the very blessings that God pours forth. It is therefore an inward evil, not a simple jealousy, against which protection is asked — a sickness that gnaws at the one who carries it as much as it aims at the one it touches.

mots.def: coordinating conjunction: and.
preposition: from, out of; here, the idea of protection against.
evil, harm; that which is bad or harmful.
ḥāsid: the envier, the one who wishes another to lose the good he holds; active participle of the verb ḥasada.
temporal particle: when, at the moment when envy becomes an act.
ḥasada: to envy; to wish another to be deprived of a good or a grace he has received.
